metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the A→B→C transitivity test (or add a sibling test) that: after drift propagates to C, re-reviews plan B with unchanged content, and asserts plan C's `review-plan --status` returns 0 (current), proving re-review stops further propagation.
evidence: test_natural_a_to_b_to_c_invalidation_requires_each_narrow_material_change ends after asserting stale-pin-drift propagates; it does not re-review plan_b and confirm plan_c's pin status returns to current
Search for 're-review stopping propagation', 'unchanged re-review' across tests returns no matches
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'successful unchanged re-review produces no further propagation' (AC #3 and Implementation list) but no test in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` demonstrates this; the A→B→C transitivity test only proves propagation occurs, not that a fresh re-review of B stops C from being invalidated.
findings.location: AC #3: 'An unchanged successful re-review produces no further propagation.' / Implementation list: 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation'
scenarios: A re-review of plan B without any plan change could inadvertently clear C's stale pin even though C's plan still references old material — or it could fail to clear it — neither case is validated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a public-surface lifecycle test that edits a ticket's plan while it is `in_progress` (execution phase) and asserts the close gate correctly reflects the self-edit (stale pin or phase mismatch), or explicitly remove this scenario from the Implementation list.
evidence: Search for 'self-edit', 'self_edit', 'execution_time' in test_plan_policy_dogfood.py and tests/interfaces/lifecycle returns no matches
No function in test_plan_policy_dogfood.py (lines 1–490) describes an execution-phase self-edit scenario
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'execution-time self-edit proven through public lifecycle surfaces' (Implementation list) but no test in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` or any lifecycle file covers this scenario.
scenarios: An agent edits the ticket plan while it is in_progress (execution phase) — it is unverified whether the material-pin check rejects a subsequent close or whether the plan-review attestation is correctly invalidated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add lifecycle tests for (a) unchanged successful re-review producing no further downstream propagation (AC3) and (b) execution-time self-edit of the active ticket not invalidating the in-flight attestation (AC5 execution adaptation).
evidence: Plan states: 'Add public-surface lifecycle scenarios for: ... successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation; execution-time self-edit'

Exhaustive search of tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_policy_dogfood.py and all other lifecycle test files finds no test function referencing unchanged re-review, re-review stopping propagation, self-edit, or execution-time adaptation.

AC2 states: 'End-to-end tests prove direct and natural-transitive invalidation only after actual narrow material changes' — the A-B-C test covers the positive invalidation side but not the 'no-propagation-on-unchanged-re-review' side.
AC3 states: 'An unchanged successful re-review produces no further propagation' — no test asserts this.
findings.finding: Two Implementation-listed lifecycle scenarios — 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation' and 'execution-time self-edit' — have no covering test anywhere in the test suite.
findings.location: Implementation section, bullet: 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation; execution-time self-edit'
scenarios: AC3 ('An unchanged successful re-review produces no further propagation') would fail a test audit since no test exercises it.
AC5 ('Execution adaptation ... proven through public lifecycle surfaces') has no test for the execution-time self-edit sub-scenario.
findings.suggested_fix: In test_plan_policy_dogfood.py, add: (a) a test that calls review_plan twice on the same unchanged ticket and asserts the second call returns PASS and that a downstream dependent's review-plan --status remains exit 0 (no further propagation); (b) a test that edits the ticket description while in_progress (simulating an execution-phase adaptation) and asserts that the existing execution-phase attestation remains valid at close.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add dogfood test functions for 'execution-time self-edit blocks close' and 'unchanged re-review does not propagate invalidation' to `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` so AC3 and the full AC5 scope are covered under both policies enabled.
evidence: Implementation: 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation; execution-time self-edit'
AC3: 'An unchanged successful re-review produces no further propagation.'
AC5: 'Execution adaptation, return-to-open reset, and code-only close freshness are proven through public lifecycle surfaces.'

test_plan_policy_dogfood.py has `test_in_progress_to_open_resets_phase_and_requires_a_planning_review` (return-to-open) and `test_close_allows_code_only_head_drift_after_execution_review` (code-only), but no test for 'execution-time self-edit' (editing the plan while in_progress invalidates the execution attestation) or 'unchanged re-review stops propagation'.
findings.finding: Two more scenarios named in the Implementation section and implicitly required by AC3/AC5 — 'successful unchanged re-review stopping propagation' and 'execution-time self-edit' — have no test functions in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py`. The resign/re-review staleness guard is mechanically correct and unit-covered elsewhere, but the plan commits to proving these behaviors through the dogfood file's public lifecycle surfaces with both rebar policies enabled.
findings.location: Implementation section (listed scenarios) / Acceptance Criteria lines 3 and 5 / tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_policy_dogfood.py
scenarios: An agent edits the plan body after claiming (in_progress) but before the execution review; the resulting stale attestation should block close — this is unproven in the dogfood context.
After a re-review on an unchanged plan, downstream C's pin status should remain current (no propagation spike) — this is asserted in AC3 but has no test function.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each of AC8's five negative assertions (e.g., `git diff --name-only HEAD | grep -E 'migration|schema' | wc -l` asserts 0 new migration files; a `rebar list` baseline snapshot diff asserts no output change; `rebar --help | grep -v pin-command-name` or similar asserts no new pin command surface).
evidence: 'Verify no eager backfill, graph-wide invalidation event, new pin command, ordinary-list output change, or stored relation migration occurs.' — Implementation section
AC8: 'No eager backfill, graph-wide invalidation event, new pin command, ordinary-list output change, or stored relation migration is introduced.' — no linked step or command
Verification section covers only affirmative behaviors (cases 1-4); no negative-assertion checks are listed
findings.finding: AC8's five negative assertions — no eager backfill, no graph-wide invalidation event, no new pin command, no ordinary-list output change, no stored relation migration — have no concrete proving command or artifact that produces evidence of absence. The plan's Implementation bullet says 'Verify no eager backfill…' and the AC checkbox repeats the list, but neither section names a command, schema diff check, migration file glob, or structured output comparison that would confirm each property holds after the change.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8; Implementation — 'Verify no eager backfill…' bullet
scenarios: An executor completes all four CLI cases and the full test suite but cannot demonstrate that, e.g., the tracker DB has no new migration file or that list output is byte-identical to baseline — the criterion is technically unverifiable as written.
findings.suggested_fix:
In the Verification section, add a block such as:
```
# AC8 negative-assertion checks
# (1) No stored-relation migration: assert no new migration files
git diff --name-only origin/main | grep -cE '(migration|schema_version)' | xargs -I{} test {} -eq 0
# (2) No ordinary-list output change: capture baseline before enabling flags, compare after
diff <(rebar list --format json | jq 'sort') <(rebar list --format json | jq 'sort')
# (3) No new pin command: assert 'pin' is not a top-level subcommand
rebar --help | grep -v 'pin'
# (4) No graph-wide invalidation: assert invalidation event count <= pre-change count (from test fixture telemetry)
```
Adjust commands to match actual CLI surface; the key requirement is one deterministic command per negative claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the `expected_reason_tokens` values (even `[]` if none are required) for all four Verification cases explicitly in the plan, so the gate-health assertion is unambiguous for every case.
evidence: 'Each case contains … expected_reason_tokens, observed_reason' — Verification section
Case (1) and case (2) prose describe expected exit and status but name no reason tokens
Case (3): 'names the canonical stale target plus stale-pin-drift' — implicitly defines tokens
Case (4): 'instructs the operator to run review-plan' — implicitly defines a token
'Gate health passes only when … every required reason token is present' — implies tokens must be pre-specified
findings.finding: The `expected_reason_tokens` for Verification cases 1 and 2 are not specified anywhere in the plan. Case 3 names 'stale-pin-drift' and 'canonical stale target' in prose; case 4 names 'run review-plan'. An executor recording the JSON object for cases 1 and 2 must guess what tokens to populate (or leave the field empty), making the gate-health check inconsistently applied across the four cases.
findings.location: Verification section — JSON record schema and four case descriptions
scenarios: Executor records empty expected_reason_tokens for cases 1 and 2, then trivially satisfies 'every required reason token is present' (vacuously true for empty set), weakening the gate-health check.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to each case description: e.g., case (1): `expected_reason_tokens: []`; case (2): `expected_reason_tokens: []`; case (3): `expected_reason_tokens: ["stale-pin-drift", "<canonical-stale-target-id>"]`; case (4): `expected_reason_tokens: ["review-plan"]`. This makes the gate-health check non-vacuous for all four cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion covering the CHANGELOG.md and docs/release-notes.md updates (e.g. 'CHANGELOG.md and docs/release-notes.md record the rebar-project policy cutover for both enforcement keys').
evidence: 'updates CHANGELOG.md plus docs/release-notes.md for the rebar-project policy cutover' — named deliverables
No AC references CHANGELOG.md or docs/release-notes.md
findings.finding: The Implementation section describes two documentation deliverables (updating CHANGELOG.md and docs/release-notes.md for the rebar-project policy cutover) but no Acceptance Criterion covers them — they are orphan deliverables with no measurable done-state.
findings.location: Implementation section, last paragraph: 'updates CHANGELOG.md plus docs/release-notes.md for the rebar-project policy cutover'
scenarios: A reviewer could consider the story done without updating docs; the deliverable has no gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'CHANGELOG.md and docs/release-notes.md are updated with an entry describing the rebar policy cutover (enforce_plan_material_pins and require_plan_review_for_close set to true).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out path for rebar.toml (e.g. 'If enforcement causes unexpected rejections on live tasks, revert both keys to false in rebar.toml and re-run make check to confirm gates are clear') so reversal is a documented, not ad-hoc, operation.
evidence: 'Enable verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close in rebar.toml'
No mention of reverting rebar.toml or a back-out procedure if enforcement blocks live work
The plan protects library consumers ('retain false defaults') but not the rebar project itself from enforcement-induced blockage
findings.finding: Enabling both enforcement policies in rebar.toml changes live task-lifecycle behavior for the rebar project itself, but the plan specifies no rollback or back-out path if the enforcement causes unexpected rejections on in-flight rebar tasks.
findings.location: Implementation: 'Enable verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close in rebar.toml'; Verification: 'both policies loaded as true'
scenarios: An in-flight rebar task with a stale pin is rejected at close after both flags are enabled, blocking real project work with no documented reversal procedure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under Implementation: 'To revert: set both enforce_plan_material_pins and require_plan_review_for_close to false in rebar.toml and open a follow-up story to diagnose the rejection before re-enabling.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan note specifying that the newly-enabled enforcement gates emit at least one observable signal (structured log line, metric counter, or named error event) for each rejection and for any unexpected internal error, so operators can distinguish a policy rejection from a gate malfunction.
evidence: Plan specifies expected exit codes (0 and 1) and reason tokens in CLI output but names no metric, log event, or alert for enforcement gate failures.
ABSENCE: no mention of structured logging, metric emission, or alerting for the stale-pin-drift or missing-review rejection paths once policies are live.
Verification records results in a JSON ticket comment with no fallback if that write fails.
findings.finding: The plan enables two new enforcement policy gates in rebar.toml (enforce_plan_material_pins, require_plan_review_for_close) but specifies no observability instrumentation — no metrics, structured logs, or alerts — for the newly active failure points. Operators cannot distinguish an enforcement rejection from a system error in the enforcement machinery itself.
findings.location: Verification section / Implementation bullet: 'Enable verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close in rebar.toml'
scenarios: An enforcement-gate internal error (e.g., pin-resolution logic panics) silently presents as an opaque exit 1 indistinguishable from a legitimate rejection, blocking a valid close with no operator-visible cause.
Policy is enabled in production (rebar.toml) and starts rejecting; on-call has no signal to determine whether rejections are legitimate or a bug in the enforcement path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (or confirm sibling stories specify) the atomicity primitive — lock, optimistic version field, or DB transaction — that makes the pin-freshness-check-then-status-write sequence free of TOCTOU races, and confirm the concurrent-recheck test scenario validates this guarantee end-to-end.
evidence: Plan says concurrency is 'proven through public lifecycle surfaces' but names no atomicity primitive (lock, CAS, transaction) protecting the pin-check-then-status-write sequence.
ABSENCE: no description of what the expected outcome is when two concurrent close attempts both read a fresh pin and race to write closed status — is the second idempotent, rejected, or undefined?
Sibling stories are named as owners of enforcement logic, so if atomicity is not specified there either, the gap is real.
findings.finding: The plan lists 'concurrent review/close changes' as a scenario to prove through public lifecycle surfaces but does not specify the atomicity mechanism (lock, CAS, transaction, or optimistic version check) used to prevent check-then-act TOCTOU races when two concurrent agents simultaneously pass the pin-freshness check and then both attempt to transition status.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Deleted and archived target handling, concurrency rechecks, and audited force-close are proven through public lifecycle surfaces.'
scenarios: Two agents concurrently pass the pin-freshness check (both read pins as fresh) then both attempt to write closed; without an atomic compare-and-swap on status, both succeed, producing a double-close event or masking a mid-flight material change.
A retry of a failed close re-reads pins after a concurrent material change has occurred between the original check and the retry, silently passing a now-stale check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a public-surface lifecycle test in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` (or a dedicated file) that proves concurrency recheck behaviour with both policies enabled, or explicitly descope the claim and remove it from AC #7.
evidence: test_plan_policy_dogfood.py ends at line 490 with no concurrency test function
Search for 'concurrent review close', 'concurrency recheck', 'concurrent' in tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_policy_dogfood.py returned no matches
No lifecycle test file contains a concurrency scenario for review/close with both policies enabled
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'concurrent review/close changes proven through public lifecycle surfaces' (AC #7 and Implementation list) but no such test exists in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` (file ends at line 490) or any other lifecycle file.
findings.location: Implementation section / AC #7: 'Deleted and archived target handling, concurrency rechecks, and audited force-close are proven through public lifecycle surfaces.'
scenarios: Two agents race to close the same ticket after a pin update — gate behaviour under concurrent writes is unverified and could silently pass or double-sign.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dogfood test in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` that invokes `force-close` on a ticket with stale material pins while both `enforce_plan_material_pins` and `require_plan_review_for_close` are enabled, and asserts exit 0, `closed` status, and the `FORCE_CLOSE` audit comment.
evidence: Search for 'force-close', 'force_close' in test_plan_policy_dogfood.py returns no matches
test_close_gate_story_epic.py:test_force_close_still_closes_with_audit_comment does not enable enforce_plan_material_pins
test_plan_review_close_gate_lifecycle.py:test_force_close_bypasses_plan_gate uses monkeypatching and does not enable the material-pin policy
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'audited force-close proven through public lifecycle surfaces' (AC #7, Implementation list) with both policies enabled, but `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` contains no force-close test. Existing force-close tests (`test_close_gate_story_epic.py`, `test_plan_review_close_gate_lifecycle.py`) do not enable `enforce_plan_material_pins`, so the audited path with both policies simultaneously active is untested.
scenarios: An operator force-closes a ticket with stale pins while both policies are on — the audit comment and bypass path are unverified in this combined-policy context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend or add a test that exercises force_close through the real close path and asserts a FORCE_CLOSE audit comment appears in the ticket's event history (e.g. via rebar.show_ticket or rebar.deps inspecting comments).
evidence: Plan says: 'audited force-close are proven through public lifecycle surfaces'
AC7: 'Deleted and archived target handling, concurrency rechecks, and audited force-close are proven through public lifecycle surfaces.'

test_force_close_bypasses_plan_gate monkeypatches gate_check to raise AssertionError, then calls rebar.transition(..., force_close='approved'). The monkeypatch causes the real close_ticket path to be entered, but the FORCE_CLOSE audit comment (transition_close.py lines 690-706) writes a comment after the status transition — no assertion on comments exists in this test.
Searching all tests for 'FORCE_CLOSE', 'force_close.*comment', 'audit.*comment', 'audit_comment' yields zero matches.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'audited force-close' is 'proven through public lifecycle surfaces' (Implementation + AC7), but the existing test (test_force_close_bypasses_plan_gate) only proves the gate is skipped — it does not verify the FORCE_CLOSE audit comment is written to the ticket history through the real close path.
scenarios: If the audit comment write is silently dropped (exception swallowed at line 701), the test would still pass, meaning the 'audited' aspect of force-close is unverified.
AC7 is partially satisfied (gate-bypass is proven) but the durable-audit-signal aspect is not proven.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add dogfood test functions to `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py` (or an adjacent file in the same fixture) for (a) concurrent-recheck under both policies enabled using the real CLI and (b) audited force-close that records the audit comment, so AC7 is fully satisfied through public lifecycle surfaces.
evidence: AC7: 'Deleted and archived target handling, concurrency rechecks, and audited force-close are proven through public lifecycle surfaces.'
Implementation: '…concurrent review/close changes; and audited force-close.'
test_plan_policy_dogfood.py ends at line 490 — no `test_concurrent_*` or `test_force_close_*` function appears in the file.
Existing concurrent-recheck coverage uses monkeypatch (test_plan_review_close_gate_lifecycle.py:47) not the real CLI under both-policies-enabled conditions.
findings.finding: Three scenarios that AC7 commits to proving 'through public lifecycle surfaces' — (a) concurrent review/close changes and (b) audited force-close — have no corresponding test functions in `test_plan_policy_dogfood.py`, which ends at line 490 with the deleted-target test. The mechanism for the concurrent recheck exists in `transition_close.py` and is unit-covered by `test_locked_recheck_detects_change_after_precheck`, but that test uses `monkeypatch` on the gate function, not the real CLI with both policies enabled. The audited force-close has coverage in `test_plan_review_close_gate_lifecycle.py` and `test_completion_gate.py` but again under different fixture conditions than the dogfood file requires.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 7 / Implementation section / tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_policy_dogfood.py (entire file, line 490 ends with no further tests)
scenarios: A race between a review invalidation and a concurrent close attempt is not exercised under the dogfood fixture, so the plan's own AC is not met by the described implementation.
Force-close audit comment is not verified in the context where both policy flags are true, leaving a gap between what the plan claims and what the test file delivers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence to the Implementation or Goal section stating the positive rationale for the subprocess-CLI-over-fixture-repo approach (e.g., 'A subprocess fixture repo is used rather than library calls because it exercises the real CLI exit-code contract and config-loading path that agents observe in production').
evidence: Goal: 'Prove the integrated behavior through public surfaces' — describes what, not why.
Verification: 'invoke the real checkout CLI with no `--dry-run` bypass' — describes how, not why the fixture-repo approach was chosen.
No sentence in the plan explains why subprocess CLI over library API, or why fixture rebar.toml over real rebar.toml mutation.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the chosen approach (isolated fixture repo + subprocess CLI + FakeRunner). The Goal says 'Prove the integrated behavior through public surfaces' but does not articulate WHY this surface (subprocess CLI against a fixture repo) was preferred over library-call integration tests or a real-project mutation approach — leaving the implementer without a basis for resolving ambiguity if scope questions arise.
scenarios: An implementer uncertain whether to extend existing library-level lifecycle tests or add new subprocess-CLI tests has no rationale to anchor the decision.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'The subprocess-CLI-against-fixture-repo approach is used (rather than library-only tests) because it exercises the exact CLI exit codes, config-loading path, and error messages that agents and operators observe — the public surface the goal names.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
